title: Government policies to achieve efficient resource allocation and correct market failure
paperName: Cambridge International A Level Economics (P3 & P4)
summary: 9708 A Level — taxes, subsidies, regulation, and tradable permits with GeoGebra welfare diagrams.
learningObjectives: Evaluate taxes and subsidies for externalities.

simpleExplanation.title: Fixing market failure
simpleExplanation.summary: Pigouvian taxes align private costs with social costs. Subsidies correct positive externalities. Each tool has limitations.
steps: Tax shifts MPC up to MSC for negative externality.
Permits cap total pollution — trade creates efficiency.
Government failure: poor information, lobbying, bureaucracy.

flashcards.back: A tax set equal to the marginal external cost so that MPC shifts up to MSC — internalising the negative externality.
Shifts MPB up toward MSB (e.g. education, vaccinations) — raises output toward the socially optimal level.
Cap total emissions; firms trade permits — low-cost abaters sell to high-cost abaters, achieving the cap at minimum cost.
Regulation sets standards directly; market-based tools (taxes, permits) use price signals — often more cost-effective.
Intervention that fails to improve allocation — imperfect information, bureaucracy, lobbying, or unintended side effects.

flashcards.front: When is a tax preferred to regulation?
flashcards.back: When marginal external cost is measurable and firms differ in abatement costs — tax preserves flexibility; regulation may force uniform costly compliance.

sections.content: **Government policy** (8.1) follows **externalities** (7.4). Evaluation essays need **two policies compared** with **diagrams** — show welfare triangles gained and any remaining deadweight loss.

items: **Pigouvian tax:** set tax = MEC at optimal output; diagram shows MPC shifting up to MSC.
**Permits:** cap-and-trade achieves target at lower cost than uniform regulation.
**Government failure:** compare remaining market failure with cost of intervention.
**Evaluation:** effectiveness depends on information, enforcement, and political economy.

sections.question:
A chemical plant emits pollution with a marginal external cost of $4 per unit at the current output of 10 000 units. Private marginal cost is $12; marginal social cost is $16.

(a) Using a diagram, explain how a Pigouvian tax could correct this market failure.
(b) Evaluate whether tradable permits might be more effective than a tax in this industry. [12 marks]
sections.solution:
(a) **Diagram:** Draw MPC at $12 and MSC at $16 (parallel, MSC above). Free market produces where MPC = MPB — **overproduction** with deadweight welfare loss.

**Pigouvian tax of $4 per unit** shifts MPC up to MSC. New equilibrium is at socially optimal output where MSC = MSB. Welfare gain = triangle between old and new output on MSC–MPB diagram.

(b) **Tax advantages:** Simple to administer if MEC is known; continuous incentive to cut emissions below the taxed level.

**Permit advantages:** **Certainty over total emissions** (hard cap); firms with low abatement costs sell permits — **cost-effective** allocation. Better when MEC varies across firms or is hard to measure.

**Judgement:** If the government prioritises a **fixed emissions target**, permits may dominate; if the goal is **price certainty** for firms, a tax may be preferred. Both beat command-and-control if abatement costs differ across plants.

steps.caption: Tax on negative externality — shift MPC up toward MSC.
Subsidy on positive externality — shift MPB up toward MSB.
Regulation, tradable permits, public provision alternatives.
Government failure: imperfect info, bureaucracy, unintended effects.
